exemplar.title: How does a country's Human Development Index (HDI) correlate with the Availability, Affordability, Quality, and Sustainability as measured by the Global Food Security Index (GFSI), within a set of 113 countries?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does a country's Human Development Index (HDI) correlate with the Availability, Affordability, Quality, and Sustainability as measured by the Global Food Security Index (GFSI), within a set of 113 countries?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report is well structured with clear headings, a table of contents and consistent formatting.
weaknesses: Sections contain wordiness and minor typographical issues.
Some table headers are empty and plot axes lack clear labels, affecting readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall organization and use of terminology are good, but lapses in concision and clarity in figures/references limit this to the 2-mark level.

strengths: Research question is precise and focused, specifying variables, population and statistical relationship.
Context section defines food security, cites current statistics and connects the issue to socioeconomic development.
weaknesses: Limited discussion of ecological consequences (e.g., land degradation, biodiversity loss).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student formulates a coherent, narrowly focused research question and situates it within a wellâexplained environmental context. Minor gaps in ecological framing prevent a top mark, placing this criterion solidly in the 5 band.

strengths: Methodology is fully repeatable, detailing data sources, statistical tests and software.
Variables table clearly organizes dependent, independent and control factors.
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks justification for excluding certain countries.
Risk assessment does not include a systematic matrix or mitigation strategies.
Data cleaning and handling of missing entries are not described.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design of a secondaryâdata protocol is appropriate and repeatable, but omissions in sampling rationale, detailed risk mitigation and dataâcleaning procedures limit full credit, warranting a bandâ4 score.

strengths: Evaluation links conclusions to postâpandemic conditions and political factors.
Discussion references external literature (e.g., Senâs purchasingâpower theory) to interpret findings.
weaknesses: Outlier discussion is descriptive without quantitative sensitivity analysis.
Proposed improvements (e.g., 5âyear study) remain superficial without methodological detail.
Strengths and weaknesses of the method are identified but not explored in depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Some evaluation and contextual reflection are present, but limited depth in methodological critique and improvement suggestions restrict this to the lower band.

strengths: Full raw dataset and descriptive statistics are presented, fostering transparency.
Correct use of ShapiroâWilk tests and a clear Spearman correlation matrix.
weaknesses: Scatter plots for HDI versus each GFSI pillar are missing.
P-values and confidence intervals for correlation coefficients are not reported.
Qualitative labels are not annotated beside coefficients.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis are largely correct, but key omissionsâvisual inspection plots and statistical significance measuresâlimit completeness, placing the work in the 3âmark band.

strengths: Application proposal is clearly linked to findings and justified through environmental and socioeconomic benefits.
weaknesses: Solution evaluation omits discussion of scalability, monitoring mechanisms and ecological tradeâoffs.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student justifies a relevant application based on results, but the evaluation of strengths and weaknesses is partial, aligning with a midâband performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific terms (HDI, Spearman coefficient, food availability) are used accurately, but sections occasionally contain wordiness and minor typographical issues, so concision is not fully consistent.

The evaluation comments on how 2022 post-pandemic conditions may skew findings and notes political factors influencing availability.  These points link the conclusion back to the environmental issue, but coverage is brief and omits quantitative re-assessment, so some omissions remain.

The conclusion restates the pattern (strong to very strong positive correlations) and relates it to socioeconomic mechanisms, clearly supported by the coefficients presented.  Some over-generalisation about causality remains, so the strand meets, but does not exceed, the 3-4 descriptor.

Normality tests are applied, and the selection of Spearman correlation is appropriate.  Correlation coefficients are calculated and interpreted with a qualitative scale.  While p-values and confidence intervals are not reported, the analysis is largely correct, placing it in the âcorrect but incompleteâ band.

One improvement (extend study to a 5-year period) and acknowledgement of external shocks are offered, representing more than a superficial tweak but still limited in scope (no methodological enhancements like multivariate controls).  This fits the upper end of the 1-2 band.

A fully repeatable secondary-data protocol is outlined: data sources (UNDP, Economist GFSI 2022), statistical tests (Shapiro-Wilk, Spearman/Pearson), software (Jamovi) and planned visualisations.  These steps are appropriate to obtain sufficient data for the correlation study; only finer details of data cleaning are missing, so the strand fits the upper band.

Links between HDI and each GFSI component are explained through purchasing-power arguments and references to conflict, governance and sustainability.  The student moves beyond listing by analysing mechanisms and feedback loops, showing comprehensive understanding.  A few assertions lack quantitative evidence but overall the connections are thoroughly explained.

The student states a precise, measurable question that clearly identifies both variables (HDI and four GFSI pillars), specifies the population (113 countries) and the statistical relationship to be examined (correlation).  The wording is coherent and narrowly focused; no ambiguity exists about scope or intent.  Consequently, the strand satisfies the ârelevant, coherent and focusedâ descriptor.

The student weighs benefits (employment, reduced emissions) against costs (government expense, risk of cheaper imports undermining producers).  Several relevant strengths and weaknesses are analysed, but factors such as monitoring, scalability, or unintended ecological impacts are omitted, so evaluation is partial.

Clear headings, a table of contents, logical sequencing and consistent formatting make the report easy to navigate from start to finish, satisfying the âwell structured and organized throughoutâ requirement.

Strengths (large global dataset) and a principal weakness (single-year snapshot) are identified, yet other limitationsâsuch as potential multicollinearity or the secondary nature of dataâare not analysed in depth.  Thus, the discussion is present but not exhaustive.

The context section discusses the global rise in food insecurity, cites current statistics, defines food security and its four GFSI dimensions, and connects these to socioeconomic development.  Sources are provided, demonstrating awareness of the broader environmental issue driving the investigation.  Minor gaps (e.g., limited treatment of ecological consequences) prevent a perfect score but the discussion clearly meets the âdiscussesâ level.

The sample is described as â113 countriesâ drawn from the 2022 GFSI dataset, but the rationale for including or excluding nations is not justified (e.g., why 113 instead of all 125 ranked).  Nevertheless, the choice is at least described, placing the work in the âdescribes the choiceâ level rather than mere outline.

Potential data bias and ethical risks of stigmatising low-HDI nations are identified, along with reliability of sources.  The discussion is more than a list but lacks a systematic matrix of likelihood/severity or mitigation steps, so it corresponds to an âoutlineâ rather than a comprehensive description.

Raw data tables, descriptive-statistics table and box-plots for all variables are included and correctly labelled.  However, scatter plots of HDI versus each GFSI pillar (crucial for correlation) are absent, representing a minor omission, so the work sits in the 3-4 band.

The proposalâsupporting sustainable local food systems in Sub-Saharan Africaâis clearly linked to the findings (low HDI/GFSI scores) and justified through environmental, social and economic benefits.  The reasoning is explicit, meeting the top descriptor.

Arguments generally progress logically from data to interpretation, but there are points where narrative digresses (e.g., repeated references) causing some difficulty for the reader.  Hence it is mainly logical yet not fully coherent, matching the 2-mark descriptor.

annotations.comment: The research question is precise, specifying both variables (HDI and GFSI pillars), the population (113 countries), and the statistical relationship, demonstrating excellent focus and coherence.
Consider stating a directional hypothesis (e.g., expecting positive correlations) to sharpen the inquiry and guide statistical selection more explicitly.
The context section effectively defines food security and cites current statistics, grounding the investigation in a realâworld environmental issue.
Expand the context by discussing ecological consequences of food insecurity (e.g., land degradation or biodiversity loss) to enrich the environmental framing.
The methodology section clearly outlines a repeatable secondaryâdata protocol, including data sources, software, and statistical tests.
Add a description of data cleaning procedures and how missing or inconsistent entries will be handled to strengthen method reliability.
The sampling strategy mentions 113 countries but lacks justification for excluding other ranked nations; a rationale for this choice should be provided.
Risk assessment outlines potential biases and ethical pitfalls but does not include a severity/likelihood matrix or mitigation measures.
Clarify how âCountriesâ are treated as controlled variablesâdetail the criteria ensuring comparability (e.g., region, population) to avoid confounding.
The variables table neatly organizes dependent, independent, and control factors, aiding reader understanding of the study design.
Including the full raw dataset fosters transparency and reproducibility by allowing verification of each data point.
Several header cells in the raw data table are empty, which disrupts readability; ensure all columns have clear labels.
Descriptive statistics and the ShapiroâWilk tests are presented correctly, demonstrating a solid initial data analysis.
The report omits pâvalues or confidence intervals for the Spearman correlations, limiting the ability to assess statistical significance.
Include scatter plots of HDI versus each GFSI pillar to visually inspect linearity and potential outliers before correlation analysis.
The Spearman correlation matrix is clearly formatted and properly labels coefficients with sample sizes, aiding interpretation.
Statistical significance (pâvalues) of the Spearman coefficients is not indicated, making it difficult to confirm the strength of the observed relationships.
Annotate correlation results with qualitative labels (e.g., âstrong,â âvery strongâ) directly beside coefficients to link findings to your grading scale.
Box plots effectively reveal medians, interquartile ranges, and outliers, supporting discussion of distributional differences across countries.
Axes on the box plots lack clear variable labels and units, which can impede the readerâs understanding of the data displayed.
Link the box plot observations to quantitative results (e.g., explain how outliers impact the correlation coefficient) to deepen the discussion.
The discussion integrates Senâs purchasingâpower theory and WFP reports, demonstrating excellent application of external literature to interpret findings.
Outlier discussion (Yemen, Syria) is descriptive but does not quantify their influence on overall correlation; consider a sensitivity analysis.
Quantify the effect of extreme cases (e.g., compute correlation with and without outliers) to assess their impact on your results.
The evaluation acknowledges the limitation of singleâyear data postâCOVID and rightly recommends a multiâyear analysis.
Proposing a 5âyear timeframe is helpful but remains superficial; you could outline specific timeâseries methods or controls for external shocks.
Applications section aligns recommendations with findings on low HDI regions and justifies support for local food systems effectively.
The solution evaluation omits discussion of scalability, monitoring mechanisms, and potential ecological tradeâoffs, limiting its comprehensiveness.
Discuss potential policy instruments or partnerships to offset government costs when supporting local producers, enhancing feasibility.
